frameworks.name: signal detection theory (meta-d')
frameworks.one_line: Quantifies metacognitive sensitivity separately from type-1 perceptual sensitivity.

frameworks.name: magnocellular dysfunction in schizophrenia
frameworks.one_line: Motivates hypothesis that low spatial frequency processing differs in patients.

frameworks.name: multivariate fMRI decoding of confidence in DLPFC
frameworks.one_line: Neural readout of perceptual confidence as a function of spatial frequency.

core_question: Does spatial frequency of visual stimuli atypically modulate metacognitive performance in schizophrenia patients?
key_assumptions: schizophrenia involves altered magnocellular (low-SF) visual processing
yes/no detection responses can be analyzed separately for metacognitive asymmetries
manipulations: spatial frequency of grating stimuli (high vs low SF)
response type (yes- vs no-responses) in detection
key_findings: Controls showed a yes-response metacognitive advantage only for high-SF stimuli
Patients showed yes-response advantage for both high- and low-SF stimuli
DLPFC confidence decoding was higher for high-SF than low-SF in controls but SF-independent in patients
interpretations: Schizophrenia is associated with atypical spatial-frequency dependence of metacognition
Altered low-level visual (magnocellular) processing propagates to higher-order metacognitive computations
